text: The surgeon uses a bipolar fenestrated instrument in the left arm, a hook in the right arm, and a cat ear retractor in the posterior arm to dissect the fissure. The pulmonary artery (PA) is exposed by opening the adventitia, revealing the high takeoff of the apical segment posteriorly and the basilar segment anteriorly.
The surgeon navigates around the apical segment, carefully sloping the basilar segmental arteries while avoiding the lingular artery. The descending thoracic aorta is visible in the background.

The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to carefully staple the lung tissue while preserving the lingular artery. The lung is lifted to expose the vein, followed by dissection in the inferior pulmonary ligament to harvest the station line and lymph nodes.

The surgical view reveals the descending thoracic aorta posteriorly, the esophagus medial to the base of the pulmonary ligament, and the pericardium with the underlying left ventricle visibly beating. The left atrium is located proximal to the pulmonary vein.
The surgeon carefully teases off the posterior hiloprura along with the adventitia of the vein, using a loop to maneuver around these structures.

The surgeon utilizes wristed robotic instruments for smooth articulation, introducing the stapler posteriorly through two twelve millimeter ports to ensure safe and flexible access during the pulmonary lobectomy procedure.
The surgeon flips the lung back down and proceeds to staple the lower lobe bronchus after dissecting a few lymph nodes.

The surgeon introduces an endocatch bag through the anterior port to remove the left lower lobe along with the specimen. The surgical field shows the extracted lobe being carefully placed into the retrieval bag.
